notes: Mizpah and Tabor, both mountains, were famous for hunting; hence, the "net" and "snare." Revolters were those who drove animals into a pit that had been camouflaged. The metaphor depicts the rulers and priesthood in the bloody role of the hunters who spiritually killed their prey, Israel. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 107)
JPS: And they that fall away are gone deep in making slaughter; and I am rejected of them all.
JPS: Their doings will not suffer them to return unto their God; for the spirit of harlotry is within them, and they know not the LORD.

Israel ought to have begotten children of God in the maintenance of the covenant with the Lord; but in its apostasy from God it had begotten an adulterous generation, children whom the Lord could not acknowledge as His own (Keil and Delitzsch, Commentary, 10:1:89).
trumpet (Heb. chatsotserah) from chatsar "to sound a trumpet"
Ramah =" hill" a place of battle between Israel and Syria

Deuteronomy 27:17 says, "Cursed be he that removeth his neighbour's landmark" (see also Deuteronomy 19:14). Because there were no fences in ancient Israel, a man's property was marked with stone markers or "landmarks." To move such a mark was a serious offense, for it was the same as stealing a man's land. If one who destroyed his neighbor's boundaries was cursed, how much more cursed were the princes of Judah who destroyed the moral and spiritual boundaries that guarded the worship of Jehovah? In Hosea 5:11 the phrase "walked after the commandment" indicates that Ephraim was oppressed because it willingly walked after filth instead of walking after true commandments (see Hosea 5:11n). (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 107)

The Prophet Joseph quoted from Hosea 6 to show that the second coming of Christ would not be before the 1890s. (Teachings, p. 286.) Unfortunately, his reasoning was not recorded and the relevance of his statement is now past. (Monte S. Nyman, "Restoring 'Plain and Precious Parts': The Role of Latter-day Scriptures in Helping Us Understand the Bible," Ensign, Dec. 1981, 24)
